vulnerabilities.description: REST API endpoints have no authentication. Any client can submit tasks, approve/reject gates, ingest documents, and query sensitive data.
vulnerabilities.cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function
vulnerabilities.impact: Unauthenticated attacker can submit arbitrary legal tasks, approve malicious findings, ingest manipulated documents, and access all stored information.
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Implement API key authentication, OAuth2, or JWT tokens on all REST endpoints. Require per-operation authorization checks. Use middleware like Fastify's @fastify/jwt or custom auth handler.

vulnerabilities.description: Task descriptions and round goals are directly interpolated into model prompts without sanitization. Attackers can inject instructions via task descriptions.
vulnerabilities.cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code
vulnerabilities.impact: Prompt injection attacks can manipulate agent behavior, exfiltrate documents from knowledge store, approve malicious findings, or generate false legal opinions.
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Sanitize or escape task descriptions and round goals before interpolating into prompts. Use separate system/user roles strictly. Consider using prompt-level schema validation (Claude's structured outputs feature).

vulnerabilities.description: Finding content is directly used in debate protocol prompts without sanitization. Malicious findings can inject instructions into the resolver model.
vulnerabilities.cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code
vulnerabilities.impact: An earlier agent (or ingested document) can craft findings that manipulate the debate/verification process, overturning correct findings or upholding incorrect ones.
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Quote or escape finding content before passing to debate models. Use structured outputs for debate results. Validate finding format and content constraints before processing.

vulnerabilities.description: Document content from ingested PDFs is chunked and directly embedded in search results. No validation of content before use in agent prompts.
vulnerabilities.cwe: CWE-95: Improper Neutralization of Directives in Dynamically Evaluated Code
vulnerabilities.impact: A malicious PDF ingested into the knowledge store can contain prompt injection payloads that will be fed to agents in subsequent rounds, compromising all downstream analysis.
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Validate and sanitize document content on ingestion. Consider scanning for prompt injection patterns. Use document attestation/signature verification. Apply content security policies to documents.

vulnerabilities.description: parseJsonObject() uses regex to extract and parse JSON without schema validation. Could accept malformed or injection-laden JSON.
vulnerabilities.cwe: CWE-91: XML Injection (or JSON injection by analogy)
vulnerabilities.impact: Malformed JSON in model responses could bypass intended output structure, allowing agents to escape their constraints.
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Use strict JSON schema validation with libraries like zod or ajv. Validate parsed JSON against expected schema before using. Return errors if validation fails.

vulnerabilities.description: Template loading from src/templates/ directory uses extname and readFile without path validation. If templates can be user-controlled, path traversal is possible.
vulnerabilities.cwe: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory
vulnerabilities.impact: An attacker could load arbitrary files from the filesystem (e.g., /etc/passwd, .env files) if template paths are user-controlled.
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Whitelist allowed template IDs. Use path.resolve() and path.normalize() to ensure paths stay within src/templates/. Never accept user-supplied file paths directly.

vulnerabilities.description: Ollama embedding endpoint is hardcoded but Config.local.ollamaUrl is user-configurable. Could be redirected to internal services via env var injection.
vulnerabilities.impact: If OLLAMA_URL is controlled by attacker, they can redirect embedding calls to internal APIs, Metadata services (AWS/GCP), or internal tools.
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Validate OLLAMA_URL against a whitelist of allowed hosts. Use DNS validation. Disable SSRF-prone protocols (gopher, file://, etc.) with a URL parser. Consider network isolation.

vulnerabilities.description: Audit log (audit.jsonl) is append-only and grows unbounded. No size limits, rotation, or cleanup policy visible. Full task descriptions and findings are logged.
vulnerabilities.impact: Disk exhaustion attack: submit large tasks to fill disk. Sensitive legal information accumulates in unencrypted log file indefinitely.
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Implement log rotation (daily, size-based). Set max file size limits. Encrypt sensitive fields in audit log. Implement retention policy (e.g., purge after 90 days). Consider write-once append-only semantics with encryption.

vulnerabilities.description: Audit events are emitted on EventEmitter and SSE-streamed to any connected client without authentication. Real-time legal work details are exposed.
vulnerabilities.cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
vulnerabilities.impact: Unauthenticated attacker can subscribe to /audit/stream SSE endpoint and monitor all legal tasks in real-time, learning about client matters.
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Require authentication on SSE endpoints. Filter audit stream per-user (only show their tasks). Redact sensitive fields (document content, findings) from real-time stream. Use WebSocket auth.

vulnerabilities.description: Config object is exported and accessible throughout the codebase. Embedding API key (OpenAI_API_KEY) is accessible if local embeddings disabled.
vulnerabilities.impact: If Config object is logged, serialized, or exposed in error messages, API keys could leak. Config also exposed to all agents via context.
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Never export full Config object. Create a filtered Config getter that redacts secrets. Use a SecureConfig wrapper. Pass only required (non-secret) settings to agents.

vulnerabilities.description: approve_gate and reject_gate operations have no user/role checking. Any authenticated user (if auth is added) can approve/reject any gate, including for other users' tasks.
vulnerabilities.cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key
vulnerabilities.impact: A reviewer can approve malicious findings for other users' cases, or reject legitimate findings, compromising legal work integrity.
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Implement fine-grained authorization: only task owner or assigned reviewers can approve gates. Audit all gate approvals with reviewer identity. Use role-based access control (RBAC).

vulnerabilities.description: No rate limiting on REST API endpoints. An attacker can DoS the service by submitting unlimited tasks or ingesting huge documents.
vulnerabilities.cwe: CWE-770: Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling
vulnerabilities.impact: Attacker floods service with tasks, consuming LLM API quota, Qdrant vector DB space, and disk (audit logs), causing denial of service.
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Implement rate limiting per IP and per user (once auth added). Use Fastify's @fastify/rate-limit. Limit task size, document size, API call frequency. Implement request queuing.

vulnerabilities.description: ingest_document tool accepts arbitrary content size with no validation. Massive documents can be ingested, causing memory/storage exhaustion or slow down Qdrant.
vulnerabilities.impact: Attacker uploads gigabyte PDF, causing out-of-memory errors in PDF processing, Qdrant overload, or service crash.
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Enforce max document size (e.g., 100MB). Validate file type on upload. Implement async document processing with progress tracking. Use chunking with bounded chunk size.

vulnerabilities.description: Vite proxy proxies all backend routes to localhost:3101 with changeOrigin: true. No CORS headers or origin validation visible.
vulnerabilities.cwe: CWE-942: Permissive Cross-domain Policy with Untrusted Domains
vulnerabilities.impact: If the UI is served from different origin, CORS may be misconfigured, allowing cross-origin document access.
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Explicitly configure CORS with whitelist of allowed origins. Do not use changeOrigin without validation. Set proper CORS headers in Fastify backend. Use @fastify/cors.

vulnerabilities.description: Infisical bootstrap vars (INFISICAL_CLIENT_SECRET) must be in .env file. If .env is accidentally committed, master credentials are leaked.
vulnerabilities.impact: If .env is committed to git, attacker gains access to all Infisical secrets (API keys, passwords, etc.).
vulnerabilities.recommendation: .env must be in .gitignore. Use .env.example with placeholders only. Consider pre-commit hooks to prevent .env commits. Use environment-specific secret injection in CI/CD.

vulnerabilities.description: Agents have allowedTools list but tool invocation has no runtime permission checks. An agent could attempt to call tools not in its allowedTools array.
vulnerabilities.cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key
vulnerabilities.impact: A compromised agent prompt could trick the agent into calling unauthorized tools (e.g., drafting agent calling signature tools), or exfiltrating documents.
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Implement strict tool authorization at tool invocation time. Check tool name against allowedTools before execution. Reject unknown tools. Log all tool calls with agent identity.

summary: Big Michael has critical authentication gaps (no API auth), high prompt injection risks (task/finding content in prompts), and uncontrolled resource consumption vulnerabilities. Legal task data is exposed to unauthenticated access and real-time monitoring. Immediate remediation required before production use.
topThreats: Unauthenticated REST API enables task submission, data exfiltration, and gate manipulation
Prompt injection via task descriptions and ingested documents can compromise legal analysis and findings
No authorization on sensitive operations (gate approval) allows unauthorized decisions on legal matters
recommendations: CRITICAL: Implement mandatory authentication (OAuth2/JWT) on all REST endpoints and MCP server before production.
CRITICAL: Add input sanitization for task descriptions, findings, and document content used in model prompts. Use Claude's structured outputs to constrain agent outputs.
CRITICAL: Implement fine-grained authorization per task, with audit trail of who approved/rejected gates.
HIGH: Add rate limiting and request size limits to prevent DoS via task/document flooding.
HIGH: Validate and whitelist template paths; use path normalization to prevent directory traversal.
HIGH: Encrypt sensitive fields in audit log; implement log rotation and retention policies.
HIGH: Require authentication on all SSE streaming endpoints; filter streams per-user.
MEDIUM: Validate Ollama/embedding URLs against whitelist to prevent SSRF attacks.
MEDIUM: Implement tool authorization checks at invocation time; never call tools outside allowedTools.
MEDIUM: Add CORS configuration with origin whitelist; remove permissive changeOrigin settings.
LOW: Redact secrets from Config exports; use SecureConfig wrapper for agent context.
